plan: Fuse multiple bitonic-sort stages into a single kernel launch using shared memory for the innermost stages. When seq_len fits within a thread block (seq_len <= 2*BLOCK_SIZE), load a tile of data into shared memory, perform all remaining stages for that tile entirely in shared memory (no global round-trips), then write back. This eliminates hundreds of kernel launches and global memory round-trips for the small-seq phases. Implement by adding a second kernel (e.g., `bitonic_sort_shared`) that loops over stages internally using `__syncthreads()` after each compare-swap pass. The outer host loop calls this fused kernel once per step for stages that fit in smem, falling back to the existing per-stage global kernel only when seq_len > block coverage. Be careful: shared memory tile size must be exactly 2*blockDim.x ints; padding may be needed if BLOCK_SIZE changes. Correctness risk: `__syncthreads()` must appear after every compare-swap pass before re-reading, not just at tile boundaries. Performance risk: smem bank conflicts if stride-1 access is not ensured—lay out the tile so each warp accesses consecutive 32-bit words.

Eliminate uncoalesced global memory accesses (currently 50% load efficiency) by rewriting the per-thread index arithmetic so that threads within a warp always access consecutive memory locations. In the current kernel, thread i accesses a[i] and a[i+h_len]; when h_len is large (e.g., 8M for the outermost stage), the two accesses are in entirely different cache lines but that is unavoidable—however for mid-range stages the strided pattern causes wasted transactions. Replace the flat thread-to-element mapping with a warp-aware mapping: divide the array into chunks of 2*h_len, assign consecutive threads to the first half of each chunk, so thread t accesses a[t] and a[t + h_len] where t is laid out such that the low half and high half of each chunk are each contiguous across warps. Specifically, compute `int half_block = h_len < blockDim.x ? h_len : blockDim.x; int group = i / half_block; int lane = i % half_block; int base = group * 2 * half_block; int lo = base + lane; int hi = lo + half_block;` and use lo/hi instead of i/swapped_ele. Verify that seq_num and odd/increasing logic is updated consistently. Correctness risk: off-by-one in group/lane math; validate with small n (n=4) before testing n=24.

Reduce kernel launch overhead and CPU-side `pow()` cost by replacing the double nested host loop (300 launches for n=24) with a single persistent-kernel or batched approach for the global stages. Concretely: (a) Replace all `pow(2, x)` calls in the host loop with integer bit-shifts (`1 << x`) to eliminate FP-to-int conversion. (b) Pass seq_len and two_power as compile-time template parameters for the most common values, or pack both into a single 64-bit argument to reduce kernel argument overhead. (c) Alternatively, encode the full sequence of (seq_len, two_power) pairs into a small device-side lookup table and launch a single grid-stride kernel that iterates over the table with `__syncthreads()`-equivalent barriers via cooperative groups (`grid.sync()`), requiring `cudaLaunchCooperativeKernel`. This cuts 300 kernel launches to 1. Correctness risk with cooperative groups: the entire grid must fit in the resident block count of the device; query `cudaOccupancyMaxActiveBlocksPerMultiprocessor` and size the grid accordingly. Without cooperative groups, at minimum apply (a) and (b) to reduce per-launch overhead.

Increase thread-level memory-latency hiding by raising the block size and tuning occupancy. Currently BLOCK_SIZE=256 with 16 registers/thread gives ~80% occupancy on sm_89; however the 59% memory/long-scoreboard stall rate suggests more in-flight warps would help. Try BLOCK_SIZE=512 or BLOCK_SIZE=1024 to increase warps-per-SM and better hide DRAM latency. Additionally, add `__launch_bounds__(BLOCK_SIZE, 2)` to the kernel to give the compiler a hint to limit register pressure and allow more concurrent blocks. Also annotate the two global loads with `__ldg()` (read-only cache via texture path) since d_input is only written by the same kernel in a previous launch: `int ai = __ldg(&a[i]); int asw = __ldg(&a[swapped_ele]);`. This routes loads through the 128-byte read-only cache, improving effective bandwidth for repeated accesses to the same cache lines across warps. Correctness risk: `__ldg` is only safe if the loaded locations are not being written by threads in the same kernel launch—confirm that each launch only writes to non-overlapping pairs.

Overlap CPU serial sort with GPU execution using CUDA streams and async memory transfers to hide the CPU BitonicSort latency. Currently the host calls `cudaDeviceSynchronize()` before running `BitonicSort`, so the CPU is idle during GPU execution and the GPU is idle during CPU sort. Restructure `ParallelBitonicSort` to: (1) use `cudaMemcpyAsync` with a non-default stream for the H2D copy; (2) launch all kernels into the same stream; (3) issue `cudaMemcpyAsync` (D2H) into the stream but do NOT synchronize yet; (4) return immediately. Then in `main`, start `BitonicSort(data_cpu, n)` on the CPU immediately after `ParallelBitonicSort` returns, and only call `cudaStreamSynchronize` after the CPU sort finishes. This hides the CPU sort time (~tens of ms for n=24) behind the GPU. Additionally, pin `data_gpu` with `cudaMallocHost` instead of `malloc` to enable true async DMA for the H2D/D2H transfers. Correctness risk: the D2H memcpy must complete before `memcmp`; ensure `cudaStreamSynchronize` is called before the comparison. This plan does not reduce raw GPU time but reduces total wall-clock time.
